title: Stanzaic Morte Arthur
summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME XML for University of Michigan item AHA2659.0001.001. U-M encoded J. Douglas Bruce's 1903 EETS volume as uncorrected OCR at TEI in Libraries Level 1, but the verbal XML boundary is only the title page and Stanzaic Morte Arthur on printed pp. 1–121; introduction, notes, glossary, index, and other carrier matter are represented only by omission placeholders and page links. Bruce explicitly based his text on F. J. Furnivall's 1864 edition, retained Furnivall's line numbering, and used repeated manuscript collations to correct it, so the graph records Bruce as version_of Furnivall and collated_against Harley MS 2252 rather than as a newly asserted continuous transcription. Furnivall states that his text is an accurate rendering read twice against Harley. Harley ff. 86r–133v is the one witness in current DIMEV and is called a unique copy by the British Library and Database of Middle English Romance, but a missing leaf between ff. 102 and 103 creates an internal lacuna. A later Benson/Foster edition is based mainly on Bruce and was checked against an unidentified microfilm; that unresolved reproduction remains between the later edition and the manuscript. Cached Bruce, Furnivall, DIMEV, and Benson/Foster deliverables remain distinct from the currently unavailable British Library manuscript images, and all rights and access layers are separately recorded.
review_notes: The cataloged work is the anonymous Stanzaic Morte Arthur, not the composite Harley commonplace book, Bruce's full EETS volume, the later two-work Benson/Foster volume, Malory's prose work, or the distinct Alliterative Morte Arthure.
Every one of the 3,849 XML L elements is a direct child of an LG under the sole BODY poem division; no L is nested in a note, title page, front matter, or back matter.

The raw 3,849 L count includes fifteen non-verbal editorial rows. Removing them leaves 3,834 textual verse entries, matching DIMEV; one of those is the bracketed supplied line 1413b, absent from Harley, so 3,833 entries transcribe extant manuscript verse.

Furnivall's main numbering jumps from 1181 to 1318, reserving 136 integer positions for the lacuna, and ends at 3969. One unnumbered gap-marker L and fifteen b/c-suffixed L entries make the terminal number exceed the raw XML count by exactly 120; the 3969 repertory figure is therefore conventional lineation, not a tag or physical-line count.

The 482 XML LG elements are Bruce's editorial stanza arrangement. Direct L sizes are 479×8, 1×7, 1×6, and 1×4; after editorial placeholders are removed, textual sizes are 471×8, 2×7, 8×6, and 1×4. Furnivall says the manuscript writes the lines continuously, while DIMEV and MERM support the eight-line stanza analysis.

The current British Library catalog dates copying of the target poem to the second half of the fifteenth century. Its early-sixteenth-century compilation, London association, 1460–1540 aggregate range, and mixed English/Latin language fields describe the composite host and are not transferred wholesale to the poem.

The cached Benson/Foster PDF is a 297-page image-only scan of a 1994 printed edition containing two distinct poems. Stanzaic Morte Arthur occupies PDF pages 16–128 / printed pp. 11–123; the Alliterative Morte Arthure begins at PDF page 134 and is outside aha2659.

The one-witness claim is bounded to the current British Library record, DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 record 3252, and the current Database of Middle English Romance. No fragments are listed in those sources; this is not a universal guarantee against future discoveries.

Rights for the abstract medieval work, physical witness, Furnivall and Bruce editions, scans and OCR, U-M encoding, repository XML, DIMEV data, METS print/digital layers, and any British Library reproduction are distinct.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2659
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The exact file is 208,272 bytes.
Its four DIV1 containers are two omitted-front placeholders, one poem, and one omitted-back placeholder; only the poem division contains L elements.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2659.0001.001
entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR encoding at TEI in Libraries Level 1. It verbally retains the Bruce title page and poem, supplies placeholders for omitted front and back matter, and links 180 page-image positions across the full carrier.
notes: All 3,849 L elements belong to the poem; fifteen are editorial placeholder rows rather than verse wording.
The inherited N='3311' between 3407 and 3415 is an encoding typo for Bruce's printed 3411 and is retained as source evidence rather than silently corrected in the artifact.

entities.label: J. Douglas Bruce's 1903 Le Morte Arthur
alternate_labels: Le Morte Arthur: A Romance in Stanzas of Eight Lines
entities.description: The immediate scholarly edition behind the U-M encoding. Bruce based the text on Furnivall's 1864 edition, retained Furnivall's numbering, repeatedly collated the manuscript to correct readings, and newly arranged the poem into eight-line stanzas. The target poem is printed pp. 1–121 inside a xxx + 148-page volume with introduction, notes, glossary, and index.

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Extra Series

bibliographic.citation: J. Douglas Bruce, ed., Le Morte Arthur, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 88 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1903).
bibliographic.title: Le Morte Arthur: A Romance in Stanzas of Eight Lines
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 88
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

notes: The header note that another EETS title is bound with the source copy describes the physical carrier and does not enlarge the work boundary.
Bruce's inherited numbering reserves 136 integer positions between 1181 and 1318, but Bruce argues that one missing leaf likely contained only 84 or 92 lines.

entities.label: F. J. Furnivall's 1864 Le Morte Arthur
entities.description: The prior edition used as Bruce's textual base. Furnivall says the text is otherwise an accurate rendering of Harley MS 2252, with which it was read twice; he expanded contractions, added punctuation and other reader aids, supplied line 1413b, and numbered across a presumed loss of one or more leaves.

bibliographic.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., Le Morte Arthur, with a prefatory essay on Arthur by Herbert Coleridge (London and Cambridge: Macmillan and Co., 1864).
bibliographic.title: Le Morte Arthur

notes: The Internet Archive item's metadata incorrectly gives Bruce's 1903 title, creator, publisher, and date while its internal title page and contents are Furnivall 1864. The provider conflict is preserved rather than silently normalized.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2252, ff. 86r–133v
alternate_labels: The Commonplace Book of John Colyns
entities.description: The one witness listed in DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 and called a unique copy by the current British Library and Database of Middle English Romance. The target occupies the second of two late-fifteenth-century booklets at ff. 86r–133v. A missing quire leaf between ff. 102 and 103 creates an internal textual lacuna; the poem otherwise has an opening and explicit.

date_statements.label: copying of Ipomydon B and Stanzaic Morte Arthur booklets
date_statements.value: second half of the fifteenth century
date_statements.notes: The whole composite manuscript was assembled and augmented by John Colyns in the early sixteenth century; that later layer does not redetermine the target poem's copying date.

holding.notes: The target is ff. 86r–133v. The manuscript was bought by John Colyns in 1517, later entered the Harley collection, and became a foundation British Museum/British Library manuscript.

physical_description.extent: Composite volume; target poem ff. 86r–133v. The catalog's quire viii is 16-1, with the second leaf missing.
physical_description.format: Composite commonplace book made from two Middle English literary booklets and later early-sixteenth-century additions
notes: The target was copied by two fifteenth-century scribes; Bruce places the change after f. 101v.
The British Library records English Gothic cursive scripts for the composite manuscript.
The aggregate codex contains Middle English and Latin, but the target poem is Middle English.
The physical manuscript is extant even though one target leaf is missing and its legacy online images are currently unavailable.
No manuscript facsimile is contained in the cached Benson/Foster PDF.

entities.label: Anonymous Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur
entities.description: The conceptual Middle English poem, distinct from its sole currently recorded physical witness and from all editorial and digital representations. The one witness preserves the beginning and ending but has an internal missing-leaf lacuna.

date_statements.label: composition
date_statements.value: competing estimates of c. 1350 and about the end of the fourteenth century
date_statements.notes: The proposals remain attributed and unresolved.

entities.label: La Mort Artu
entities.description: The Old French Arthurian prose romance described by modern METS as the narrative condensed in the Stanzaic Morte Arthur. The entity represents a conceptual source work, not an identified physical exemplar; Bruce's analysis posits mediated and changing source relationships rather than direct copying of one surviving French manuscript.

entities.label: Internet Archive/Google scan of Bruce 1903
entities.description: A complete 189-page image-container PDF of the 1903 Bruce volume. The poem is PDF pages 36–156 / Internet Archive leaves n35–n155 / printed pp. 1–121.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 189-page image-container PDF, scanned by Google and hosted by Internet Archive

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR derivatives for Bruce 1903
entities.description: Complete ABBYY-derived plain-text and DjVu XML OCR of the 189-page Bruce scan.

entities.label: Internet Archive/Google scan of Furnivall 1864
entities.description: A complete 253-page image-container PDF whose internal title page identifies Furnivall's 1864 edition. The poem is PDF pages 62–223 / Internet Archive leaves n61–n222 / printed pp. 1–162. Internet Archive's external title/creator/date metadata incorrectly identifies Bruce 1903.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 253-page image-container PDF, scanned by Google from the University of Michigan copy and hosted by Internet Archive

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR derivatives for Furnivall 1864
entities.description: Complete ABBYY-derived plain-text and DjVu XML OCR of the 253-page Furnivall scan, notwithstanding the host metadata conflict.

entities.label: British Library Archives and Manuscripts record 040-002048083
entities.description: The current official description of the composite manuscript, its exact Stanzaic Morte Arthur locus, unique-copy status, target copying date, physical collation, provenance, currently unavailable digital images, and request routes.

entities.label: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 record 3252
entities.description: The exact versioned DIMEV work and witness record for Stanzaic Morte Arthur: NIMEV 1994, eight-line abababab stanzas, 3,834 lines, slightly imperfect, one Harley witness at ff. 86r–133v, incipit, explicit, and edition list.

entities.label: Database of Middle English Romance record for Stanzaic Morte Arthur
entities.description: The current romance and Harley manuscript pages, recording title, c. 1350 and North Midlands proposals, stanza form, plot, one unique copy, conventional 3969-line extent, and modern editions.

entities.label: Benson/Foster 1994 King Arthur's Death
entities.description: A pedagogically regularized two-work edition containing Stanzaic Morte Arthur on printed pp. 11–123 and the distinct Alliterative Morte Arthure later in the volume. Benson says the stanzaic text is based mainly on Bruce and was read against a microfilm of Harley; Foster revised and updated the 1994 edition.

bibliographic.citation: Larry D. Benson, ed., revised by Edward E. Foster, King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1994).
bibliographic.title: King Arthur's Death: The Middle English Stanzaic Morte Arthur and Alliterative Morte Arthure

notes: The text regularizes spelling and plural pronouns and adds marginal glosses; one conjectured stanzaic line is bracketed and excluded from the line numbering.
This later edition is supporting evidence, not upstream of the U-M encoding.

entities.label: METS digital encoding of Benson/Foster 1994
entities.description: The current Middle English Texts Series HTML/XML digital text derived from the Benson/Foster printed edition. Its three reader parts cover Stanzaic Morte Arthur and its notes; this encoding is distinct from both the 1994 scholarly-edition entity and the downloadable image-only PDF facsimile.
notes: No METS XML deliverable was cached during this research; the accessible reader presentation and the provider's XML license establish the digital layer without equating it with the PDF.

entities.label: Unidentified Harley MS 2252 microfilm consulted by Larry D. Benson
entities.description: The manuscript microfilm against which Benson says he read Bruce's stanzaic text. The preface does not identify its producer, generation, holding institution, reel number, date, exact coverage, or present survival.
entities.reproduction_format: Microfilm; exact format and coverage unresolved
notes: This intermediary prevents a false direct Benson-to-codex relation.
It is not the cached METS PDF and is not assumed to be the British Library legacy digital surrogate.

entities.label: METS downloadable PDF scan of Benson/Foster 1994
entities.description: The complete current 297-page image-only downloadable PDF of the printed two-work edition. Stanzaic Morte Arthur is PDF pages 16–128; notes continue through PDF page 133; Alliterative Morte Arthure begins at PDF page 134.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 297-page image-only PDF scan of the printed 1994 edition
notes: This entity is a facsimile of a modern printed edition, not a facsimile of Harley MS 2252.

access.access_method: Exact current BL catalog, legacy ARK, BL IIIF, Internet Archive shelfmark, and broad facsimile searches found no working legitimate complete public surrogate. This is a dated negative finding, not proof of permanent non-digitization.
Registered or recently renewed readers may request Harley MS 2252 before visiting; manuscripts and archives must be consulted at the Library in London.

rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed in the fourteenth century and survives in a fifteenth-century witness; any copyright in the medieval verbal work has expired.
Ownership and access to a physical manuscript are separate from copyright in the medieval text and from rights or provider terms for modern photography.

The pinned repository root contains the Unlicense, but its operative text repeatedly describes software and no path-specific statement expressly establishes that inherited U-M XML, data, or markup is within that dedication. The exact license evidence is retained, while coverage of this artifact remains unresolved and cannot override rights in the upstream encoding or edition.

rights.component: U-M digital item and displayed materials
rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the materials are in the public domain and displays the DPLA/RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.
U-M states that the item is public domain/No Copyright–United States, and Internet Archive metadata marks the Bruce item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the US.

The scan's Google front matter describes the book as public domain, and Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the US. Internal evidence identifies the object as Furnivall 1864 despite incorrect host cataloging.

rights.component: Google-scanned Bruce PDF and Internet Archive delivery
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and United States assertion
rights.basis: Google's scan notice treats the book as public domain but requests personal non-commercial use, attribution, no automated querying, and lawful reuse; Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the US.

rights.component: Google-scanned Furnivall PDF and Internet Archive delivery
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and United States assertion
rights.basis: Google's scan notice treats the book as public domain but requests personal non-commercial use, attribution, no automated querying, and lawful reuse; Internet Archive metadata marks the provider-conflicted item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the US.
The exact release's LICENSE and CITATION.cff declare Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.

rights.component: METS digital edition and downloadable printed-edition PDF
rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are provided for individual use only and directs users to contact the Executive Director to reprint the digital text. The cached PDF is not identified as XML and is not assigned the XML license here.
METS states that all XML is licensed CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. No METS XML file was cached for this research, and the license is not transferred to the PDF, print edition, or manuscript text.

No current item-specific image license was located. Any new reproduction is requestable by quotation; applicable copyright, contract, credit, publication, and reuse terms must be confirmed in the order response.

La Mort Artu is a medieval work; any copyright in its medieval verbal content has expired. This does not determine rights in a modern edition, translation, encoding, or facsimile, none of which is modeled as this entity.

rights.component: Internet Archive OCR derivatives of Bruce 1903
rights.basis: The underlying Bruce edition is provider-asserted public domain in the United States, but no derivative-specific license was found for the Internet Archive plain-text or DjVu XML OCR. Delivery and platform terms may apply.

rights.component: Internet Archive OCR derivatives of Furnivall 1864
rights.basis: The underlying Furnivall edition is provider-asserted public domain in the United States, but no derivative-specific license was found for the Internet Archive plain-text or DjVu XML OCR. Delivery and platform terms may apply, and the host metadata conflict remains relevant to attribution.

rights.component: Current British Library catalog expression and website delivery
rights.basis: The catalog record is a modern institutional description distinct from the public-domain medieval text. No item-specific open license for the record was identified during this research; website and database terms may apply.

rights.component: Database of Middle English Romance description and website delivery
rights.basis: The current site displays Copyright © 2026 University of York. No open license for the target record was identified; this modern catalog expression is distinct from the public-domain medieval poem.

rights.component: Unidentified microfilm photography of Harley MS 2252
rights.basis: Benson proves that a manuscript microfilm was used but gives no producer, date, repository, identifier, coverage, license, or access terms. No rights conclusion can safely be transferred from the manuscript, BL legacy ARK, or modern METS PDF.

rights.component: 1994 printed Benson/Foster scholarly edition apart from the separately licensed XML
rights.basis: METS states that print format is governed by the guidelines articulated in the edition. The 1994 editorial expression is modern and is not made public domain by the medieval source text; no broader open license for the print layer was identified.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Bruce's complete xxx + 148-page EETS volume and a separately bound title
editorial_practices.representation: Title page and poem verbally encoded; remaining carrier matter represented by omission placeholders and PB links
editorial_practices.description: U-M's verbal encoding boundary is much narrower than the scanned carrier. The poem is complete as edited by Bruce, subject to the witness lacuna; the introduction, notes, glossary, index, and unrelated bound matter are not additional encoded work parts.

editorial_practices.representation: Expanded forms italicized in the printed edition
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall states that he expanded contractions and marked expansions typographically. He also separated the article a from following nouns.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Continuous manuscript text with limited scribal punctuation
editorial_practices.representation: Editorial stops, inverted commas, hyphens, accents, and lineation
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall expressly says these reader aids were added; otherwise he calls the text an accurate rendering read twice against the manuscript.

editorial_practices.representation: Corrected readings, manuscript notes, new stanza layout, and retained Furnivall line numbers
editorial_practices.description: Bruce says repeated manuscript collations corrected errors in Furnivall. He retained Furnivall's numbering for comparison and rearranged the presentation into eight-line stanzas; the missing-leaf allowance therefore remains inherited even though Bruce thought it too large.
editorial_practices.scope: Bruce's poem text and introductory textual discussion

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source, source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2659.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: complete file; HEADER, TITLEPAGE, all DIV1/LG/L/PB/MILESTONE/NOTE1 structures
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for repository identity and hash, U-M identifier and publication/encoding statement, Bruce source edition, encoded boundary, 482 LG and 3,849 L elements, notes, line attributes, printed pages, manuscript milestones, and omission scaffolding.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped machine inspection of the pinned aha2659 XML structure, ancestry, stanza sizes, placeholders, and line numbering.
evidence.locator: all 3,849 L and 482 LG elements; every explicit N attribute; BODY/FRONT/BACK/NOTE ancestry
evidence.summary: Confirmed that all L nodes are direct LG children under BODY/DIV1 TYPE='poem'; counted 3,849 raw L, fifteen non-verbal placeholder rows, 3,834 textual verse entries, one supplied line absent from the manuscript, fifteen suffix-numbered L entries, the 1181-to-1318 discontinuity, terminal 3969, exact group-size distributions, and the inherited 3311-for-3411 typo.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AHA2659.0001.001.
evidence.locator: item information, rights, contents, and poem division
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the Bruce edition identity and date, U-M identifier, Arthurian-romance subject, public-domain/No Copyright–United States assertion, and omission/poem division structure.

evidence.citation: J. Douglas Bruce, ed., Le Morte Arthur, EETS Extra Series 88 (London, 1903).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. vii–xxx, especially ix–xii; poem pp. 1–121 = PDF pages 36–156
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for Bruce's full volume and poem boundary, Furnivall textual base, retained numbering, repeated manuscript collations, Harley folios and scribes, missing-leaf gap and 84/92-line physical estimate, eight-line stanza layout, date/dialect/source arguments, supplied and placeholder lines, and the complete ending and explicit.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and derivatives for lemortearthurar00brucgoog.
evidence.locator: complete item metadata and derivative inventory
evidence.summary: Confirms the item identifier, Bruce 1903 title and creator, 189-image inventory, Google scan source, University/public access routes, exact PDF/OCR/scandata derivative names, and NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT US assertion.

evidence.citation: Rendered boundary inspection of the cached complete Bruce 1903 PDF.
evidence.locator: PDF pages 36 and 156; printed pp. 1 and 121
evidence.summary: Confirms the poem begins on PDF page 36 / printed p. 1 and ends with conventional line 3969 and the explicit on PDF page 156 / printed p. 121. qpdf reports no syntax or stream-encoding errors.

evidence.citation: F. J. Furnivall, ed., Le Morte Arthur (London and Cambridge: Macmillan and Co., 1864).
evidence.locator: internal title page; preface pp. ix–x and xxiv–xxv; poem pp. 1–162 = PDF pages 62–223
evidence.summary: Primary evidence that the internally identified object is Furnivall 1864, edited from Harley 2252; that the text was read twice against the manuscript and called an accurate rendering apart from stated interventions; and for continuous manuscript layout, stanza irregularities, supplied line 1413b, missing-leaf numbering, expansion, punctuation, and other editorial practices.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and derivatives for lemortearthurar00furngoog.
evidence.locator: complete item metadata and derivative inventory
evidence.summary: Confirms the 253-image Google scan from the University of Michigan copy and exact PDF/OCR/scandata derivative names, while preserving the provider conflict: metadata gives Bruce 1903 title/creator/date fields although internal evidence is Furnivall 1864. It also records NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the US.

evidence.citation: Rendered title and poem-boundary inspection of the cached provider-conflicted Furnivall PDF.
evidence.locator: internal title page; PDF pages 62 and 223; printed pp. 1 and 162
evidence.summary: Confirms Furnivall's internal 1864 identity, the poem start and end, and the conventional terminal line 3969 despite the external host mismatch. qpdf reports no syntax or stream-encoding errors.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Harley MS 2252, record 040-002048083.
evidence.locator: record title and scope; ff. 86r–133v content item; date, physical description, collation, provenance, digitised-content, and access fields
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the composite-host boundary, unique Stanzaic Morte Arthur copy at ff. 86r–133v, second-half-fifteenth-century copying of the target, missing quire leaf, support and dimensions, John Colyns's later compilation and ownership, currently unavailable legacy images, and consultation/reference routes.

evidence.citation: Dated exact public-facsimile search for British Library Harley MS 2252.
evidence.locator: official record digital-content field; legacy ARK; exact BL IIIF attempt; Internet Archive shelfmark and broad web searches
evidence.summary: On 2026-07-15 the official record labeled digital images unavailable; the legacy viewer did not yield images; the exact BL IIIF manifest path returned no manifest; and no working legitimate complete public facsimile was found through exact shelfmark/ARK searches. This negative result is time-bounded and does not prove permanent non-digitization.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, The Digital Index of Middle English Verse: Data Files and Documentation, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 3252.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-3252 and wit-3252-1; data/Manuscripts.xml BLHar2252; LICENSE and CITATION.cff
evidence.summary: Exact versioned evidence for title, NIMEV 1994, 3,834 lines, slight imperfection, eight-line abababab stanza form, Arthurian-romance subjects, incipit, explicit, ff. 86r–133v, edition history, one listed Harley witness, release date, and CC BY 4.0 license.
notes: The dataset is a 2026-06-25 prerelease; witness census claims are scoped to this exact release.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Stanzaic Morte Arthur and London, British Library, MS Harley 2252.
evidence.locator: romance description and manuscript list; Harley contents at ff. 86r–133v
evidence.summary: Current supporting evidence for title, c. 1350/North Midlands proposals, eight-line stanza/rhyme form, plot, modern editions, and one unique Harley copy. Its 3969-line value is conventional terminal lineation; its early-sixteenth-century/London label describes the composite host.

evidence.citation: Larry D. Benson, ed., revised by Edward E. Foster, King Arthur's Death (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1994).
evidence.locator: preface pp. ix–xii = PDF pages 2–5; Stanzaic Morte Arthur printed pp. 11–123 = PDF pages 16–128; notes pp. 124–128 = PDF pages 129–133
evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence that the stanzaic text is based mainly on Bruce and was read against an unidentified manuscript microfilm; for regularization and a bracketed conjectural line; and supporting evidence for anonymous authorship, probable date/place, source work, plot, exact target boundary, and distinction from the Alliterative poem.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, King Arthur's Death edition page.
evidence.locator: title, editors, download and purchase links, description, publication details, and two-work contents
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for Benson and Foster, Medieval Institute Publications, 1994 revision and 1974 first edition, direct PDF download, purchase route, target/Alliterative contents separation, and METS description of the stanzaic poem as a condensation of La Mort Artu.

evidence.citation: Rendered boundary and integrity inspection of the cached METS PDF.
evidence.summary: Confirms a 297-page image-only scan of the printed edition; the preface and microfilm statement; target start on PDF 16 / printed p. 11; target end on PDF 128 / printed p. 123; notes on PDF 129–133; and Alliterative material beginning PDF 134. qpdf succeeds with linearization/hint warnings, while rendering succeeds.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright & Citation.
evidence.summary: Current policy states that digital editions are for individual use only, reprinting the digital text requires contact with the Executive Director, print is governed by edition guidelines, and all XML is CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME repository LICENSE at pinned commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: The repository-root Unlicense text dedicates software to the public domain, but it does not name the inherited CME XML path or expressly state that U-M data and markup are covered. It therefore documents a potentially relevant license without resolving this artifact's scope or any upstream rights.

open_questions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover the inherited U-M XML data and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/aha2659.xml?
next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME repository maintainers.
Check upstream U-M terms and repository history for an explicit grant covering the imported XML serialization.
Until then, retain unknown artifact-level status and do not transfer the root software license to the U-M encoding, Bruce edition, or other upstream layers.

open_questions.question: What exact microfilm of Harley MS 2252 did Larry D. Benson consult, and does it survive in an identifiable repository?
next_steps: Check Benson's papers, editorial files, acknowledgements, and publisher archives for a reel or holding identifier.
Ask the British Library and relevant university repositories whether a consultation microfilm matching the edition survives.
Do not collapse the microfilm into the BL legacy digital ARK without documentary evidence.

open_questions.question: Will the British Library restore a public full facsimile of Harley MS 2252, or can an existing full reproduction be supplied?
next_steps: Monitor the official catalog, legacy ARK, and BL IIIF services.
Submit a reference enquiry quoting Harley MS 2252 and record 040-002048083.
If necessary, request a quotation for full-manuscript imaging or exact ff. 86r–133v.
notes: The current official state is unavailable, not a claim that no images were ever made.

open_questions.question: How many original lines and stanzas were lost with the missing Harley leaf?
next_steps: Keep Furnivall's 136 reserved integer positions separate from Bruce's codicological estimate of 84 or 92 lost lines.
Consult Hissiger 1975, Noguchi 1990, and later codicological scholarship before adopting any reconstruction.
